coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with different surface states simultaneously at room temperature and their sensing applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tianze
Shi, Wei
Shuang E,
Mao, Quanxing
Chen, Xuwei
coredata.dc:description: It is a considerable challenge to develop environmental friendly, low-cost methodology for green preparation of carbon dots (CDs). Herein, CDs with different surface states are prepared using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and hydroquinone (HQ) as precursors via oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction at room temperature without additional oxidizing agents. Two CDs products (YCDs and GCDs) are obtained after separation with silica gel column chromatography based on their polarity differences. The different surface states endow these two CDs with different properties. The rich NO2 and OH groups on the surface of YCDs contribute to a narrow band gap, resulting in the red-shifted photoluminescence (PL) emission of this CDs product, making it a sensitive probe for the detection of toxic pollutant p-nitrophenol (p-NP) attributed to the inner filter effect, along with a detection limit of 0.08 μmol/L. GCDs are characterized with abundant surficial NH2 groups, and can be used as a potential probe to detect H2O content in D2O, giving a detection limit of 0.17 vol%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature
Surface state
o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD)
Hydroquinone (HQ)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL assay of p-NP with YCDs
PL assay of H2O in D2O with GCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) 3D-fluorescence topographical map of YCDs and GCDs; (C, D) UV–Vis, PL excitation and emission spectra of YCDs and GCDs; (E, F) PL decay curves of YCDs and GCDs.

(A, B) TEM images and size distribution plot of YCDs and GCDs; (C, D) AFM images and the height profile analysis along the line of YCDs and GCDs; (E) FT-IR spectra of YCDs and GCDs; (F) XPS spectra of YCDs and GCDs.
(A, B) High-resolution C 1s XPS profile of YCDs and GCDs; (C, D) High-resolution N 1s XPS profile of YCDs and GCDs; (E, F) High-resolution O 1s XPS profile of YCDs and GCDs.
(A, B) Cyclic voltammograms of YCDs and GCDs; (C, D) Energy gaps of YCDs and GCDs; (E) Energy levels of YCDs and GCDs.

(A) PL spectra of YCDs after addition of various concentrations of p-NP; (B) The relationship between the PL quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and the concentration of p-NP; Inset: the linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and p-NP concentration in the range of 0.2–50 μmol/L; (C) The absorption spectra of p-NP and PLE spectra of YCDs; (D) PL lifetime decay profiles of YCDs in the absence and presence of p-NP.

(A) PL lifetime decay profiles of GCDs in H2O and D2O; (B) PL spectral variations of GCDs in D2O upon addition of different contents of H2O (0–40 vol%); (C) The relationship between F0/F and the H2O contents in D2O.
XPS data analyses of the C 1s and N 1s spectra of two CDs.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with different surface states simultaneously at room temperature and their sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a considerable challenge to develop environmental friendly, low-cost methodology for green preparation of carbon dots (CDs). Herein, CDs with different surface states are prepared using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and hydroquinone (HQ) as precursors via oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction at room temperature without additional oxidizing agents. Two CDs products (YCDs and GCDs) are obtained after separation with silica gel column chromatography based on their polarity differences. The different surface states endow these two CDs with different properties. The rich NO2 and OH groups on the surface of YCDs contribute to a narrow band gap, resulting in the red-shifted photoluminescence (PL) emission of this CDs product, making it a sensitive probe for the detection of toxic pollutant p-nitrophenol (p-NP) attributed to the inner filter effect, along with a detection limit of 0.08 μmol/L. GCDs are characterized with abundant surficial NH2 groups, and can be used as a potential probe to detect H2O content in D2O, giving a detection limit of 0.17 vol%.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of powerful photoluminescence (PL) nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention over the past decade due to their fascinating properties, including low toxicity, excellent optical behavior, superior chemical stability, high photostability, and good biocompatibility [1–3]. The universal carbon sources, i.e., from small molecular precursors to natural bioorganic materials, and even food waste, make CDs ideal nanomaterials satisfying the Principles of Green Chemistry [4,5]. Up to now, the fabrications of CDs can be easily achieved via hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave procedures and so on [6–8]. Though facile and rapid, high energy consumption such as high temperature supply for carbonization precursors or pyrolysis is prerequisite for these fabrication procedures, which might impede their large-scale production for ensuing industrial applications. Thus, the energy-saving approach for CDs fabrications is of great significance. As a low-energy method, the preparation of CDs at room temperature has become a focus in recent years, during which excess concentrated alkali, acid or other oxidizing agents are added to the reaction system to replace the high energy consumption to induce oxidation/polymerization of carbon precursors [9–13]. Xiao et al. report the preparation of CDs at room temperature using PEG 400 as both carbon source and passivizing agent. By changing the content of added NaOH, CDs with different emissions (yellow, orange red and red) are obtained [9]. Liu et al. demonstrate that CDs are prepared by mixing dopamine and H2O2 and then adding o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) at room temperature. The obtained CDs can be used as a potential tool to study cell polarity and physiological or pathological processes related to autophagy [14]. However, the excessive oxidizing agents would induce environmental issues due to the difficulty in their completely removal after CDs preparation [10,15]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop simple, green procedures for CDs preparation at room temperature without oxidizing agents, with the aim to simplify the CDs processing and minimize the environmental impact.

The surface states of CDs have been proved to be crucial on the properties of final CDs products. Shen et al. demonstrate that the treating of CDs with reducing agents would shift the CDs PL emission and improve their quantum yield, thereby making CDs more promising in the field of bioimaging [16]. Therefore, the preparation of CDs with different surface states is very important to broaden practical application. Our previous work demonstrate that solvent-involved molecular fusion strategy is an effective way to adjust surface states of the final CDs products by adopting different reaction solvents towards the same carbon source [17]. The one-pot protocol might be an efficient alternative to endow the CDs with different surface states. Ding et al. report a simple method to prepare CDs in water, and achieve several CDs with different PL emission. It reveals that the different surface states of CDs provide the final PL emission behaviors [18].

In present work, we propose a facile green one-pot method to prepare two types of CDs with different surface states using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and hydroquinone (HQ) as precursors at room temperature without additional oxidizing agents. The crude product derived from the spontaneous oxidation/polymerization and Schiff base reaction of precursors is subjected to silica gel column chromatography, and two types of CDs (YCDs and GCDs) are obtained based on their polarity differences. These two CDs products are characterized with different surface states. There are abundant NH2 groups on the surface of GCDs, while YCDs are rich in OH and NO2 groups. High content of OH groups gives YCDs strong polarity, and the OH and NO2 groups induce the decrease of band gap, resulting in the red-shifted photoluminescence (PL) emission of YCDs compared with GCDs. The different surface states offer these two CDs with distinct spectral properties, and leads to the subsequent different sensing applications. The PL excitation (PLE) of YCDs overlap with the absorbance of pollutant p-nitrophenol (p-NP) heavily, thus the inner filter effect makes YCDs a sensitive probe in p-NP sensing, giving a detection limit low down to 0.08 μmol/L. GCDs is used as a spectral probe to detect the H2O content in D2O, and a detection limit of 0.17 vol% is achieved.

Typically, HQ (100 mg) and o-PD (300 mg) are added into 30 mL water to form a transparent homogeneous solution under ultrasonication. After reacting at room temperature for 18 h, a brown suspension is obtained. The crude product is then purified with a silica column chromatography using methylene chloride/methanol mixture (20/1) as eluents. During the separation, gradient elution technique is used by gradually increasing the polarity of the elution solvent. Meanwhile, a slow flow rate is maintained to avoid cross-contamination between different CDs. After solvent removal via rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum, two CDs products, labeled as YCDs and GCDs according to the emissions, are obtained with a yield of 21 wt% and 15 wt%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL assay of p-NP with YCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The assay of p-NP with YCDs as a PL probe is conducted by the following procedure. Typically, p-NP solutions with various concentrations are mixed with 1.5 mL of YCDs solution (10 μg/mL, prepared in a BR buffer solutions of pH 9.0). The total volume of mixtures is 2.0 mL. Subsequently, the PL spectra are recorded using an excitation at 410 nm. The selectivity of the system is investigated by adding other species into this system under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL assay of H2O in D2O with GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 25 mg GCDs is dispersed in 20 mL of D2O, followed by ultrasonication for 15 min to ensure the homogeneity of the solution. Then, different volumes of H2O are added into 50 μL of the as-prepared GCDs solution. Subsequently, D2O is added to the above solution to keep the final volume of H2O/D2O mixture at 2.0 mL. Then the PL spectra of this mixture are recorded with excitation at 440 nm.

In present study, CDs are prepared using HQ and o-PD as precursors at room temperature without special equipment and oxidant reagents. The formation of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 . HQ has two active phenolic hydroxyl groups, and is easily oxidized to p-benzoquinone in air [12]. Meanwhile, the NH2 groups of o-PD are high active, making o-PD to produce various oxidation products by oxidation/polymerization in air, including o-PD dimer, o-PD trimer, 2,3-dinitro-phenazinemethane [17,19]. Moreover, Schiff base reaction occurs between the NH2 group of o-PD/o-PD oxidation products and the CO of p-benzoquinone. The heat released by the Schiff base reaction further promote the polymerization and oxidation reactions of o-PD and HQ [12], facilitating the formation of final CDs products. As both OH groups on HQ and NH2 groups on o-PD are easily oxidized, the oxidation reactions of the precursors are in competition situation, leading to two different routes in CDs formation process, and contributing to the final two types of CDs with different surface states. In route I, the oxidation/ polymerization o-PD governs the CDs formation. The NH2 groups of o-PD is oxidized to NO2 groups, and more OH groups are inherited from HQ, endowing the final YCDs with abundant NO2 and OH groups. While in route II, there oxidation of HQ plays a leading role in CDs formation, the OH groups of HQ is consumed and more NH2 groups of o-PD is retained on the final GCDs.

Dialysis is the commonly used and efficient technique for CDs purification [20], while the obtained CDs products usually show excitation wavelength-dependent PL behaviors [21], as dialysis can only screen out CDs by size effect, and the obtained product are still a complex mixture of CDs with different physical-chemistry properties [22]. Silica gel chromatography is a recently reported technique for CDs purification according to difference on physical-chemistry properties, rather than the size of CDs, therefore, CDs products with excitation wavelength-independent PL behaviors and different emissions are usually obtained [18]. Since the as-prepared CDs are supposed to be characterized with different surface states, silica gel column chromatography technique is adopted for CDs purification in present study. As shown in Fig. S1, the crude product can be well separated into two products, suggesting the great polarity difference of these two CDs products.

Fig. 2 shows the optical properties of these two CDs. 3D-fluorescence topographical map illustrates that YCDs and GCDs both own a single emission center (Fig. 2A–B), and the emission maxima of YCDs and GCDs are at ≈555 nm and ≈546 nm, respectively. The PL quantum yields are measured to be 22.4% and 16.9% for YCDs and GCDs, respectively, using Rhodamine 6G as the reference. Both these two CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission behaviors (Fig. S2). Such PL behavior of CDs is usually caused by surface states emission [23]. In the UV–vis spectra of YCDs and GCDs, the YCDs display an absorption peaks at 258 nm in the UV region (Fig. 2C), and a single peak is detected at 253 nm with a shoulder at 285 nm for GCDs in the UV region (Fig. 2D). These peaks are assigned to the π–π* transitions of CC bonds in aromatic ring structure [18]. In addition, YCDs show a characteristic absorption peak at 420 nm, and GCDs show two absorption bands at 365 and 415 nm. The distinct absorption bands in the lower-energy region well suggest the different surface states of these two CDs [24]. There is single peak observed in the corresponding PL excitation curves of these two CDs, and the peak position is close to that of the absorption band in the lower-energy region, indicating that the PL emission of these two CDs are originated from the absorption in lower-energy region [18]. The different PL excitation spectra of two types of CDs indicate that their distinct emission peaks originate from different emission states [25]. It is found that YCDs and GCDs have extremely enormously photo-stable with continuous irradiation of a Xenon lamp for 3600 s, and display excellent PL stability after 7-day storage at room temperature (Fig. S3). The CIE color coordinates of YCDs and GCDs with correlated color temperature of 4552 and 5713 K are (0.3969, 0.5893) and (0.3213, 0.5903) (Fig. S4), which further suggest the different emission behaviors of these two types CDs. The PL decay curves are fitted by monoexponential functions with lifetimes of 2.48 and 1.75 ns for YCDs and GCDs (Fig. 2E and F). Normally, a monoexponential PL lifetime indicates that a sample contain a singlet PL center [22], and the different PL lifetimes of these two CDs further confirm their different electronic excited states and different properties [26]. The optical dissimilarity of these two CDs facilitate the deep investigation into their compositions and structures.

In order to study the effect of the structure and composition of two CDs products on the surface states and PL behavior, the CDs products are characterized by TEM, AFM, FT-IR, XPS, zeta potential, XRD and Raman spectroscopy technologies. These two CDs products are monodisperse with an average size of 20.9 and 20.7 nm (Fig. 3 A–B). Both of them are torispherical nanodots, favorably distributed and without agglomeration. The corresponding AFM images (Fig. 3C–D) indicate an average height of about 20 nm, which are in good agreement with the TEM results. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3E), YCDs show the characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to the stretching vibration of OH at 3400–3500 cm−1, the stretching vibration of NH at 3200–3400 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of CH at 2932 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CN at 1625–1635 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CC at 1500–1510 cm−1, the stretching vibration of CN at 1405 cm−1, the stretching vibration of NO2 at 1330–1360 cm−1, the in-plane bending vibration of NH at 1227 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of CO at 1125–1130 cm−1 [27,28]. The same bonds are observed in the FT-IR spectra of GCDs, such as OH (3400–3500 cm−1), NH (3200–3400 cm−1), CH (2923 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1), CN (1625–1635 cm−1), CC (1500–1510 cm−1), CN (1405 cm−1), NO2 (1330–1360 cm−1), NH (1227 cm−1) and CO (1125–1130 cm−1). FT-IR spectra show that two CDs are composed of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing aromatic structures. In addition, the present of Schiff base structure (CN band at 1625–1635 cm−1) in both CDs reveal the contribution of Schiff base reaction during CDs formation, and the NO2 groups from the oxidation of NH2 groups of o-PD [19]. As referring to the nitrogen-related groups of these two CDs, the intensity of the NH band of GCDs is stronger than that of YCDs, suggesting that GCDs inherit more amino groups from o-PD than YCDs. The peaks of NO2 and CN groups are more distinct in the FT-IR spectra of YCDs, demonstrating the presence of more NO2 and CN groups in YCDs. Moreover, the enhancement of OH bands from GCDs to YCDs is observed, which suggests the higher polarity of YCDs than GCDs, as a broad OH bands usually indicates the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds on the surface of CDs [29]. The polarity difference is consistent with the elution sequence of these two CDs from silica gel column. Fig. 3F illustrates the XPS patterns of these two CDs. The three pinnacle lies within 284 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV should be put down to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s [24,30,31]. These findings indicate that these two CDs are consisted of same elements.

High-resolution XPS is used to further investigate the chemical composition for these two CDs. Fig. 4 A shows four fitting peaks for the high resolution C1s spectrum of YCDs: CC/CC (284.4 eV), CN (285.1 eV), COH (286.0 eV) and CN/CO (288.5 eV) [23,25,32]. The C 1s bands (Fig. 4B) of GCDs can be deconvoluted into four binding energy peaks corresponding to CC/CC (284.5 eV), CN (285.1 eV), COH (286.2 eV) and CN/CO (288.5 eV). In the N 1s spectrum of YCDs (Fig. 4C), three peaks are corresponding to CNC (398.9 eV), NH2 (400.0 eV) and NO2 (406.5 eV) [18,33,34]. As shown in Fig. 4D, the N 1s spectrum of GCDs shows three peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.9 eV and 406.4 eV, corresponding to the CNC, NH2 and NO2, respectively. Herein, the presence of NO2 groups is consistent with the results of FT-IR spectra of these two CDs. The high-resolution O1s spectrum of YCDs (Fig. 4E) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.9 and 532.8 eV, ascribed to CO and CO, respectively [34]. The O 1s band of GCDs (Fig. 4F) contains two peaks at 531.9 and 532.8 eV for CO and CO, respectively. Table 1 shows the relative contents of the chemical functional groups on the surfaces of CDs derived from the corresponding integral peak areas. It indicates that there are more OH groups on the surface of YCDs, endowing YCDs with strong polarity [35]. Therefore, separation of YCDs from the crude CDs is achieved by silica gel column chromatography, which are consistent with the results of FT-IR. Surface charge analyses indicate that YCDs show a negative charge of −7.23 mV under neutral condition, and GCDs is slight positive charged with a zeta potential of +0.42 mV. The different surface charges of CDs are associated with the presence of amine and hydroxyl groups as showed previously in FTIR and XPS spectra [36]. Both YCDs and GCDs show broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22° in their XRD spectra (Fig. S5), which corresponds to the sp2-hybridized carbon consisted in CDs, typical for carbon nanomaterials [37]. The disordered structures or defects (D band) and the graphitic carbon domains (G bands) in Raman spectra are molecular picture of carbon materials. The Raman spectra of these two CD products (Fig. S6) show two peaks at 1374 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band, respectively. The high intensity of D bands indicates that there are local sp3 defects within the sp2 cluster in these two CD products [38]. The IG/ID ratio of YCDs is significantly higher than that of GCDs, suggesting that YCDs own high degree of graphitization and few defects responsible for its high PL quantum yield [34].

The characterizations indicate that the distribution of functional groups of these two CDs are different from each other, which thus result in the different surface states of the final CDs product. The difference in the distribution of functional groups might be ascribed to the following fact. Both OH and NH2 groups of the precursors are easily oxidized, and a competitive relationship exists between these two groups during CDs preparation process. When precursor o-PD acts as the reaction center, the oxidation reaction of NH2 groups is more active, resulting in the abundant NO2 groups on the final YCDs. On the contrary, the oxidation reaction of OH groups is more active when HQ become the reaction center, and more NH2 groups are inherited from o-PD, resulting in the high NH2 group content in the product of GCDs. The different CDs formation routes can be further confirmed by the element contents. The N/C ration of these two CDs derived from XPS results are 0.126 and 0.1 for YCDs and GCDs respectively. The higher N content for YCDs suggest that more o-PD participating in the formation of YCDs. On the contrary, more HQ are involved in GCDs formation, leading to a lower N/C ratio.

To calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of these two CDs, cyclic voltammetry (CV) is performed with a standard three electrode system by using acetonitrile containing saturated CDs and 0.1 M TBAPF6 as the supporting electrolyte [39]. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels in eV of CDs are estimated according to the following equations [40]:
(1)
E
LUMO
= - e
E
red
+ 4.4
(2)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g
where ELUMO is the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital, and EHOMO is the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital. Ered is the onset of oxidation and reduction potential.

Ered of the YCDs and GCDs are determined to be −0.45 V and −0.47 V, respectively (Fig. 5 A–B). The band gaps (Eg) derived from the absorption edge of YCDs and GCDs absorption spectrum are 2.39 eV and 2.61 eV, respectively (Fig. 5C–D). Therefore, ELUMO of the YCDs and GCDs are calculated to be −3.95 eV and −3.93 eV, and EHOMO are calculated to be −6.34 eV and −6.54 eV. The energy diagram (Fig. 5E) indicates that when the PL emission of CDs is red-shifted, the corresponding Eg decreases with the increase of HOMO level and the decrease of LUMO level.

It is worth investigating how the structure of CDs induces changes in the energy level and affects the PL emission. Up to now, there are two popular mechanisms for the PL origination of CDs. One is the quantum size effects affected by the carbon core [41], and the other refers to the surface state, governed by the surface functional groups [18,25]. In present study, the similar particle size of these two CDs indicate that the quantum size effect should not be the main factor determining the PL emission of final CDs. Structural characterizations demonstrate that these two CDs own different surface functional groups, therefore the surface state is considered to be the main factor governing the PL performance. Both these two CDs present pH-dependent PL behaviors (Fig. S7), which further confirms the role of surface state in the PL emission [25]. Oxygen and nitrogen-related functional groups are able to affect the PL behaviors of CDs [34,42–44]. As can be seen from Table 1, YCDs contain more OH groups than GCDs, and the increase of OH number would induce the decrease of the band gap and the reduced band gap eventually lead to the red-shift of the PL emission peak of CDs [45]. At the same time, there are abundant NO2 groups on the surface of YCDs. The NO2 groups are able to introduce new energy levels into the electronic structures, resulting in a redshift of PL emission of CDs [34]. As a consequence, we conclude that the PL emissions of these two CDs are closely related to the contents of OH and NO2 groups on their surfaces, that is, the abundant OH and NO2 groups in YCDs adjust the HOMO and LUMO energy levels, resulting in the red-shift of the PL emission of YCDs when compared with GCDs.

p-NP is extensively used in the manufacture of dyes, explosives, pesticides and pharmaceuticals [46,47]. However, as one of the most hazardous and toxic phenol compound, p-NP can cause cyanosis, drowsiness, headaches, and nausea through acute inhalation or ingestion [48]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a cost-effective, facile, and rapid method for accurate determination of the trace level of p-NP in real samples. The high contents of OH and NO2 groups offer YCDs with strong polarity and unique spectral behavior. Fig. 6 A shows the gradual decrease of PL intensity of YCDs upon the addition of pollutant p-NP from 0 to 140 μmol /L. The PL signal (F0-F)/F0 exhibits a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9978) with p-NP concentration in the range of 0.2–50 μmol /L (Fig. 6B, F0 and F are the PL intensity of YCDs prepared at 555 nm before/after the addition of p-NP). The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 0.08 μmol /L, which is much lower than the maximum allowable content of p-NP in drinking water (~0.43 μmol/L) guided by USEPA [49], and comparable to other reported probes for p-NP detection (Table S1).

The selectivity of YCDs toward p-NP sensing is evaluated and illustrate in Fig. S8. The response of YCDs to the structurally similar aromatics including o-NP, m-NP, p-MP, o-MP, m-MP, PhMe, AN, HQ, PG, p-CP and p-PD are negligible under the same sensing conditions. At the same time, metal cations such as Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ do not interfere with the p-NP sensing, indicating the favorable selectivity of YCDs to p-NP. To assess the practicability of the proposed sensing system, spiking recoveries for Xinkai River and Nan Lake water samples were investigated and listed in Table S2. The recoveries in the range of 99.4–101.6% well imply the accuracy and credibility of the YCDs-based sensing procedure.

As illustrated in Fig. 6C, the PLE spectrum of YCDs almost completely overlaps with the absorption spectrum of p-NP in pH 9.0 BR buffer, which is extensively suggested for the inner filter effect (IFE) or resonance energy transfer (FRET) [50]. For the FRET process, the occurrence of the energy/electron transfer in the excited-state is expected to substantially change the lifetime of the fluorophore [51]. YCDs shows a PL lifetime about 2.48 ns, and the addition of p-NP nearly induce any change on the PL lifetime of YCDs. The heavy overlap between the PLE spectrum of YCDs and absorption spectrum of p-NP, and the unchanged YCDs PL lifetime in the absence/presence of p-NP (Fig. 6D) well suggest that the PL quench of YCDs should be ascribed to the IFE. Due to the smaller overlap of the PLE spectrum of GCDs and the absorption spectrum of p-NP, the PL quenching effect of p-NP on GCDs is lower than that on YCDs (Fig. S9).

D2O plays an important role in nuclear industry, spectral characterization and chemical analysis [52]. However, D2O is easily contaminated by H2O due to its high hygroscopicity, which affect the further use of D2O [53]. There are still few reports for detecting H2O due to severe interference from D2O, especially using CDs as probes. Therefore, it is hopefully to develop a CDs based sensor for detecting H2O in D2O. The characterizations reveal that there are abundant NH2 groups on the surface of GCDs, and it has been demonstrated that the interactions between solvent environment and NH2 groups of CDs can pose great effect on the PL intensity of CDs [21]. Fig. 7 A illustrates the PL decay curves of GCDs prepared in H2O and D2O. The average lifetime of GCDs in H2O and D2O are 1.75 and 2.11 ns, respectively. The shorter lifetime of GCDs in H2O suggest that H2O exhibits more intensive quenching effect on the excited state of GCDs than D2O, due to the higher quenching efficacy of OH than OD oscillators [52]. The different PL response of GCDs towards H2O and D2O might provide the GCDs a potential probe in sensing H2O content in D2O, as the H2O content is a mandatory index for commercially available product D2O [52]. As shown in the PL spectra (Fig. 7B), the PL intensity of GCDs decreases with increasing the H2O content in D2O. The PL ratio F0/F is proportional to the H2O content in the range 0.5–40 vol% (Fig. 7C, F0 and F are the PL intensity of GCDs prepared in D2O at 546 nm before/after the addition of H2O), and the detection limit is derived to be 0.17 vol% at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Under the same detection conditions, YCDs exhibit lower ability to distinguish H2O from D2O than GCDs (Fig. S10), which might be attributed to the different surface states.

In summary, we have reported a facile method for the preparation of CDs with different surface states at room temperature. Compared with the traditional CDs preparation protocols requiring high temperature to pyrolyze or carbonize small organic precursor molecules, this room-temperature preparation strategy is merited with the simple procedure and energy saving, making the CDs fabrication a real green process. The different spectral properties offered by the surface states leads to the different sensing applications of these two CDs products. We believe that the method for preparing CDs with different surface states at room temperature greatly promotes the progress of the method for preparing multicolor/multiple applications CDs.

Tianze Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Wei Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Shuang E: Validation, Investigation, Resources. Quanxing Mao: Resources, Project administration. Xuwei Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial supports from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21804062, 21727811), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.02.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid conversion from common precursors to carbon dots in large scale: spectral controls, optical sensing, cellular imaging and LEDs application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Qiao, G. Chen, Q. Wen, W. Liu, J. Gao, Z. Yu, Q. Wang, Rapid conversion from common precursors to carbon dots in large scale: Spectral controls, optical sensing, cellular imaging and LEDs application, J. Colloid Interface Sci., 580 (2020) 88.
